We've got a new game concept to cook up, and it's pretty open-ended, which is both exciting and a little daunting, right? First things first, as a reflective guy, I like to get my own thoughts in order before I start bouncing ideas off others. It helps me structure things and make sure I'm not just grabbing the first thing that pops into my head. I'll probably spend a good chunk of time just scribbling down whatever comes to mind – different kinds of apocalypses, unique gameplay mechanics, even just evocative keywords.

My mind naturally gravitates to the post-apocalyptic setting, given our department. But if we can go beyond zombies or nuclear winter, what's a fresh take? Maybe the apocalypse wasn't destructive in the traditional sense, but transformative? Or maybe it's not about survival, but about thriving in a radically altered world? I'm thinking about blending elements. What if we took a post-apocalyptic setting but gave it a cozy, life-sim feel? Or made it a mystery game where you're uncovering what *really* happened, piecing together clues from a ruined world? Or even something entirely abstract, where the 'apocalypse' is more internal or philosophical.

I need to think about a core hook. What would make someone pick *our* game over the hundred others out there? Is it a unique narrative? A never-before-seen gameplay loop? An emotional connection? I'll probably focus on creating a strong central concept first – a compelling story or a truly innovative gameplay idea – and then build out the contents, slogan, and selling points from there. It's like building a house; you need a solid foundation before you worry about the paint color or the landscaping.

Now, regarding who to talk to once I've got some initial concepts brewing...

Well, Yamamoto-san is our department leader, and he's also reflective, so I know he'll appreciate a well-thought-out idea. I’ll definitely need to present something coherent to him eventually, but maybe not as my very first step. He's the one who'll be summarizing and submitting, so getting his feedback later in the process will be crucial to refine things for the final pitch.

Suzuki-kun is new, just 23. He's got a balanced work style, so he might have a good blend of fresh perspectives and practical considerations. He could be a good sounding board for really out-there ideas, helping me see if they have any broad appeal or if I'm just getting lost in my own head. Plus, he's probably more up-to-date on newer trends or what younger players are looking for. I could toss some wild ideas his way and see what sticks, what resonates.

Tanaka-san is 58 and intuitive. She's got a lot of experience, and her intuitive nature means she might just *feel* what's right or wrong about an idea quickly. She could offer a different kind of insight, perhaps pointing out emotional hooks or narrative strengths that I might overlook while I'm bogged down in mechanics. We could really sit down and dissect an idea, explore its potential pitfalls, and strengthen its core. She could help me refine the story and concept, thinking about depth and consistency.

Given my reflective nature, I'll probably start by forming a few distinct ideas myself. Then, I think my first actual consultation might be with Ito-san. Since we share a reflective style, she'd likely appreciate me coming in with some structured thoughts, and we could really dig into the nitty-gritty of a concept together. That could help me solidify one or two strong contenders. After that, I might check in with Suzuki-kun to get a fresh, broader perspective on those refined ideas. And then, once I have something really solid, I'll take it to Tanaka-san for her intuitive take on its overall feel, and finally to Yamamoto-san, our leader, to get his reflective input and ensure it's ready for him to present. I want to make sure what I bring to Yamamoto-san is as polished as possible.

Interacting with the other department... hmm, that's optional. I'll keep an eye out if there's a specific need, maybe if I'm thinking of a very particular gameplay mechanic that might align with what they're doing, but for now, I think focusing on our own department's strengths and perspectives is the most efficient way to kick this off. There's plenty of brainpower right here. (formed immediately after interacting with the start of the project)
